msContents.summary: Mahzor according to the Ashkenazi rite, for the whole year

msItem.title: מחזור מנהג אשכנז לכל השנה
Maḥzor minhag ashkenaz le-khol ha-shanah
msItem.note: Used by Daniel Goldschmidt in his edition of the High Holiday services (Jerusalem, 1970).

supportDesc.extent:
ff. 340 330 230
Between ff. 213v-214r, a small piece of parchment containing liturgical corrections in a fourteenth-century hand has been sewn onto f. 213v.

handNote.p: Copied by a scribe named Jacob יעקב, who pointed out this name in the text (ff. 7v, 46v, 64r, 83r, 224v). Vocalised by Yehudah יהודה, who left a colophon on f. 159v, and who pointed out the words Yehudah and kohen in the text (see for example ff. 185r, 247r, 267r, 270r).

Throughout the manuscript there are various liturgical notes and additions, the majority of which were supplied by a fourteenth-century owner who signed his name, Yehudah ben Yerahmeel haKohen יהודה בן ירחמיאל הכהן, on f. 9r. Paleographical similarities suggest that this early owner was probably the same person as the vocaliser of the manuscript.
There are also later additions in the rites of Posen (ff. 167r, 180r) and Frankfurt (f. 127r).
ff. 40-43 are replacements by a later hand.

history.provenance: On f. 9r, ownership inscription by Yehudah ben Yerahmeel haKohen יהודה בן ירחמיאל הכהן. On f. 168r, Meir ben Avraham haLevi מאיר בן אברהם הלוי recorded the deaths of his family members, including that of his grandfather in 1519 in ורידבורג (probably Friedberg (Hessen, Germany)), and of his father in 1559 in גלהויזן (probably Gelnhausen (Hessen, Germany)); there is also a record of the sale of the manuscript from Meir ben Avraham haLevi to מאיר ס׳׳ל in 1555; the witness was Yitsik ben Shmuel haKohen יציק בן שמואל הכהן. But if Meir ben Avraham already sold the manuscript in 1555, he would not have been able to record his father's death in it in 1559 (an honorific for the living is used for his father in the 1555 deed of sale, confirming that the date of the father's death was most likely correct); it is possible that the sale did not take place as planned.
history.acquisition: It belonged to the library of Heimann Joseph Michael (1792-1846), which was purchased en bloc (altogether 862 volumes) by the Bodleian Library in 1848 through Messrs. Asher and Co. for £1030.

recordHist.source: Manuscript description based on Catalogue of the Hebrew Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library, vol. I, by Adolf Neubauer, Oxford 1886, No. 1025, Catalogue of the Hebrew manuscripts in the Bodleian Library; Supplement of Addenda and Corrigenda to Vol. I, No. 1025, and on the data of the Institute of Microfilmed Hebrew Manuscripts, National Library of Israel with additional enhancements by the cataloguer.

availability.p: Entry to read in the Library is permitted only on presentation of a valid reader's card (for admissions procedures contact Bodleian Admissions). Contact specialcollections.enquiries@bodleian.ox.ac.uk for further information on the availability of this manuscript.

listBibl.bibl: Neubauer, Adolf, Catalogue of the Hebrew Manuscripts in the Bodleian Library (Oxford, 1886), no. 1025.

Beit-Arié, Malachi and R.A. May (eds), Catalogue of the Hebrew manuscripts in the Bodleian Library; Supplement of Addenda and Corrigenda to Vol. I (A. Neubauer’s Catalogue) (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1994), no. 1025.
